document: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy _ OAIC (Australia - Row 24)

sections.heading: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy
sections.title: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy
sections.summary: This section introduces the statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy as a legal mechanism allowing individuals to seek redress for privacy harms in court. It establishes that the tort applies beyond traditional Privacy Act entities to include individuals and other entities. The section outlines key components such as the definition of invasion, required elements for a claim, and the role of the court in determining remedies.

requirements.text: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy

sections.source_quote: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy | OAIC

sections.heading: Published: 19 June 2025
sections.title: Statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy: claims and defences
sections.summary: This section details the conditions under which an individual may claim a statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy, including the requirement that the plaintiff had a reasonable expectation of privacy. It specifies that the defendant must have invaded privacy through actions like intruding upon seclusion or misusing information. The section also outlines defences such as consent or lawful authority, and exemptions for intelligence agencies, law enforcement, and journalists.

requirements.text: An individual may have a cause of action against another person or organisation who has invaded their privacy by doing one or more of the following: intruding upon the individual's seclusion or misusing information that relates to the plaintiff
The plaintiff must have had a reasonable expectation of privacy in all circumstances
The plaintiff must demonstrate that the public interest in protecting their privacy outweighs any countervailing public interest
Consent or lawful authority is a defence
Exemptions apply to intelligence agencies, law enforcement bodies, and journalists in certain circumstances

sections.source_quote: An individual (plaintiff) may have a cause of action against another person or organisation (defendant) who has invaded their privacy by doing one or more of the following, in instances where the plaintiff would have had a reasonable expectation of privacy in all the circumstances: • intruding upon the individual's seclusion – for example, by physically intruding into their private space; • misusing information that relates to the plaintiff.

sections.heading: Timeframes
sections.title: Timeframes for statutory tort claims
sections.summary: This section specifies the timeframes within which legal proceedings for the statutory tort must be commenced. If the plaintiff is under 18 at the time of the alleged invasion, proceedings must begin before their 21st birthday. Otherwise, the timeframe is the earlier of one year after the plaintiff became aware of the invasion or three years after the invasion occurred. The section also notes that courts may grant remedies such as damages, injunctions, or orders requiring an apology.

requirements.text: Proceedings must be commenced within 1 year after the plaintiff became aware of the invasion of privacy or 3 years after the invasion occurred, whichever is earlier
If the plaintiff is under 18 at the time of the alleged invasion, proceedings must be commenced before their 21st birthday
Courts may grant remedies including damages, injunctions, or orders requiring an apology

sections.source_quote: Proceedings under the statutory tort must be commenced within certain timeframes. If a plaintiff is under 18 years of age when the alleged invasion of privacy occurred, proceedings must commence before their 21st birthday. In other circumstances, the timeframe is the earlier of: • 1 year after the day on which the plaintiff became aware of the invasion of privacy; and • 3 years after the invasion of privacy occurred.

sections.heading: Site map
sections.title: Site map for statutory tort information
sections.summary: This section provides a site map for the statutory tort for serious invasions of privacy, including links to copyright, terms and conditions, privacy policy, accessibility, and contact information. It does not contain substantive legal requirements or obligations related to data transfers or privacy practices.

requirements.text: Site map includes links to copyright, terms and conditions, privacy policy, accessibility, and contact information

sections.heading: Contact us
sections.title: Contact information for statutory tort support
sections.summary: This section provides contact details for the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC), including a phone number and operating hours. It also includes an acknowledgment of country statement and copyright information, but does not contain substantive legal requirements for data transfers or privacy practices.

requirements.text: Operating hours: Monday to Thursday 10 am to 4 pm (AEST/AEDT)
